categories.name: Web Architecture
categories.description: Core strategies and patterns for building and delivering web applications.

topics.name: Static and Server Rendering
topics.description: Strategies involving pre-built files and server-side generation.

topics.name: Performance and Hydration
topics.description: Optimizing user experience through efficient JavaScript execution and loading.

questions.question_text: What are the primary benefits of the Jamstack (SSG) approach compared to standard SSR?
questions.explanation: Jamstack/SSG offers the low-cost hosting and simplicity of a static site because files are pre-rendered, while still providing an app-like experience via hydration.
questions.hint: Focus on hosting costs and simplicity.

choices.text: It eliminates the need for any client-side JavaScript.
It provides low-cost hosting and simplicity while maintaining an app-like experience.
It allows for real-time data updates without any redeployments.
It is faster than standard static sites because it skips the storage bucket.

questions.question_text: What is considered a significant barrier or drawback to implementing Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)?
questions.explanation: ISR is complex to set up manually, often requiring a specific host like Vercel that supports the pattern out of the box.
questions.hint: Think about the effort required for self-hosting.

choices.text: It makes the website significantly slower than a standard MPA.
It is more complex to set up and typically requires specialized hosting support.
It cannot handle any dynamic data or cache invalidation.
It requires a full site redeploy for every small change in data.

questions.question_text: In the context of partial hydration, what is a common strategy used to make a component like a 'highly interactive footer' interactive?
questions.explanation: Partial hydration allows developers to wait until a user scrolls down to a component before making it interactive, reducing the initial JavaScript execution load.
questions.hint: Consider the user's physical interaction with the page.
questions.answer_text: Waiting until the user scrolls down to the component before making it interactive.

questions.question_text: How do routes function in a Single Page Application (SPA) compared to a Multi-Page Application (MPA)?
questions.explanation: In an SPA, routes do not point to a server; they are updated locally by JavaScript in the browser to make the transition feel instantaneous.
questions.hint: Does the browser ask the server for a new page on every route change?

choices.text: Each route points to a unique dynamically generated HTML file on the server.
Routes are purely cosmetic and cannot fetch additional data.
Routes are updated by JavaScript in the browser and do not point to a server.
SPAs only support a single URL and cannot have multiple routes.

questions.question_text: Which type of framework is responsible for implementing the 'Best of Both Worlds' approach by handling both server-side rendering and client-side hydration?
questions.explanation: Meta-frameworks like Next.js, Nuxt, and SvelteKit handle the initial server-side request and then hydrate to client-side JavaScript.

learning_cards.title: Rendering Definition
learning_cards.front: What is the core definition of 'rendering' in web development?
learning_cards.back: The process of turning data and code into HTML that can be seen by the end user.

learning_cards.title: Islands: Zero JS
learning_cards.front: What happens in an Islands Architecture if a page has no interactive components?
learning_cards.back: No JavaScript is ever shipped to the client, even if the UI was built with a framework like React.
